title: Ploughman's lunch
subtitle: Cheese, pickle, bread, and accompaniments — assembled not cooked
excerpt: A platter of mature cheddar, Branston pickle, crusty bread, butter, a sharp apple, spring onions, and chutney. No cooking required. The quality is in the sourcing: a good cheddar, proper bread, and a firm apple are the variables. Eaten at pub lunch tables across Britain.
sourceNotes: The ploughman's lunch, despite its rustic name, was largely a creation of the English Country Cheese Council in the 1950s and 60s — a marketing concept to boost cheese sales in pubs. The combination of bread, cheese, and pickle is ancient, but the named plated version is mid-20th century. It became a genuine British institution and is now one of the most recognised pub lunches.

content.text: The cheese should be served at room temperature, not cold from the fridge. Cold cheese has almost no flavour. Take it out 30 minutes before serving. A mature cheddar aged 18 months or more is the standard; Stilton or a regional cheese can substitute.

items.prepNote: firm, sharp variety, cored and sliced

content.text: Arrange the {{cheddar}} on a board or large plate. Add the {{branston-pickle}} in a small bowl or spooned alongside. Add the sliced {{apple}} and {{spring-onion}}. Serve the {{sourdough}} on the side with the {{unsalted-butter}} separately. The elements should be presented separately so each person can compose their own combination. Do not spread the pickle directly on the bread before serving.

The ploughman's is a midday pub meal, associated with rural England, beer gardens, and summer. It requires no cooking and no skill beyond sourcing good components. The combination of sharp cheese, sweet-acidic pickle, and neutral bread is one of the most satisfying simple meals in the British repertoire.
